access.accessMethod: Open-access scholarly chapter evaluating Caxton's possible printed French exemplars
access.entityLabel: Unidentified physical French exemplar translated by William Caxton

localCopies.label: De Bruijn 2023 chapter on Caxton's uncertain French exemplar

notes: The cached scholarly article is complete as an article; it is not a surrogate of the unidentified exemplar itself.

entities.description: The physical printed copy of Jean Bagnyon's three-book French compilation from which Caxton made his 1485 Middle English translation. Current scholarship leaves the exact edition unresolved among five early printed candidates available to him.
entities.label: Unidentified physical French exemplar translated by William Caxton
notes: Missing means not presently identified, not proven destroyed.
The five candidates are Geneva prints of 1478, c. 1479, and 1483 and two Lyon printings dated 1484-1487.
It must not be merged with Herrtage's 1483 comparison copy, the 1478 first print, Cod. Bodmer 15, or BGE Ms. fr. 188 without provenance evidence.

entities.description: An abstract work-level node for Jean Bagnyon's French prose compilation, untitled in its manuscripts, composed c. 1465-1470 for Henri Bolomier and conventionally cataloged as Histoire de Charlemagne. It connects distinct manuscripts, prints, and Caxton's unidentified physical exemplar without asserting that any surviving carrier was his copy.
entities.label: Jean Bagnyon's three-book French prose compilation conventionally called Histoire de Charlemagne
notes: Histoire de Charlemagne is a conventional modern title; the manuscripts are untitled.
The long title La conqueste du grant roy Charlemaigne is first attested in later print tradition and is not treated as an authorial original title.

relations.subjectLabel: Geneva, Bibliothèque de Genève, Ms. fr. 188

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual work is Caxton's 1485 Middle English three-book translation and adaptation, not Fierabras alone, Jean Bagnyon's French source work, Herrtage's edition, U-M's encoding, or the repository XML.

The 1880-1881 Herrtage carrier includes introduction, edited text, endnotes, and glossary. U-M's 1967 source copy encodes the title matter, introduction, and edited text through printed page 252; endnotes from pages 253-263 are integrated, and their printed pages plus the glossary on pages 265-268 are absent.

ISTC establishes one complete surviving Caxton copy and two surviving fragments. Historical descriptions calling C.10.b.9 unique are therefore interpreted as unique complete copy, not unique surviving material.

The legacy British Library complete viewer and IIIF manifest are retained as historical exact routes, but both access.bl.uk and api.bl.uk failed DNS resolution on 2026-07-14; no image payload was available to cache.

The full BnF bundle retains all 245 canvases, including covers and end matter; the textual work is mapped to canvases 9-237 and canvas 238 is the blank verso of the final printed leaf. The full Bodmer bundle retains all 261 canvases, with the work mapped to canvases 9-242 (fols. 1r-117v).

Provider image and reproduction terms remain separate from the public-domain status of the fifteenth-century texts. Local caching records research custody and does not itself authorize redistribution.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME CharlesG XML, corresponding to University of Michigan CME item CharlesG. U-M keyed the text in 1999 from the 1967 Oxford/EETS one-volume reprint of Sidney J. H. Herrtage's 1880-1881 two-part edition. Herrtage transcribed the complete British Library copy C.10.b.9 of William Caxton's Westminster print dated 1 December 1485 and compared it with British Library C.6.b.12, the 1483 Geneva French print. Caxton's Middle English work translates and adapts an unidentified physical copy of Jean Bagnyon's three-book French compilation; the editor's 1483 comparison copy, the 1478 first print, and the two surviving French manuscripts are kept distinct from that unidentified exemplar. Complete public IIIF bundles are cached for the 1478 BnF print and Fondation Martin Bodmer Cod. Bodmer 15, while current and legacy routes for the Caxton print, BGE Ms. fr. 188, commercial reproductions, and requested imaging are recorded without implying public image availability.

catalogSummary.author: Jean Bagnyon; translated and adapted by William Caxton

catalogSummary.title: The Lyf of the Noble and Crysten Prynce, Charles the Grete

catalogingSubject.label: The Lyf of the Noble and Crysten Prynce, Charles the Grete
catalogingSubject.scope_note: William Caxton's three-book Middle English prose translation and adaptation of Jean Bagnyon's French Histoire de Charlemagne, completed 18 June 1485 and first printed by Caxton at Westminster on 1 December 1485. The cataloged work includes Caxton's prologue, a chapter table, eighty-six numbered chapters, the source author's excuse and envoy within Book III, and Caxton's final explicit. It is distinct from Bagnyon's abstract French work and Caxton's unidentified printed exemplar; the surviving French manuscripts and prints; British Library C.10.b.9 and its fragments; Sidney J. H. Herrtage's 1880-1881 edition and 1967 reprint; the University of Michigan encoding; and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Caxton's prologue
contentParts.summary: Caxton explains that William Daubeny asked him to translate the French book, commends the histories of virtuous rulers, and introduces Charlemagne.

contentParts.label: Table of chapters
contentParts.summary: A prospective table organizes all eighty-six numbered chapters under three books and eight parts.

contentParts.label: Book I
contentParts.summary: Thirteen chapters in three parts recount the early French kings and Clovis's conversion, Pepin and Charlemagne's life and governance, and Charlemagne's Holy Land expedition and relics.

contentParts.label: Book I, Part I
contentParts.summary: Five chapters on Clodoneus, Clotildis, Clovis's conversion and victories, and his baptism by Saint Remigius.

contentParts.label: Book I, Part II
contentParts.summary: Five chapters on Pepin's accession and Charlemagne's body, household, education, learning, governance, and charity.

contentParts.label: Book I, Part III
contentParts.summary: Three chapters on the patriarch of Jerusalem's appeal, Charlemagne's expedition to the Holy Land, and the relics he brings from Constantinople and Jerusalem.

contentParts.label: Book II
contentParts.summary: Forty-nine chapters in three parts narrate Oliver's combat with Fierabras, the captivity and rescue of the peers, Floripas's aid and the recovery of relics, and Charlemagne's campaign against Balan.

contentParts.label: Book II, Part I
contentParts.summary: Sixteen chapters centered on Oliver's single combat with Fierabras, Fierabras's defeat and conversion, and Oliver's capture.

contentParts.label: Book II, Part II
contentParts.summary: Seventeen chapters on the peers' imprisonment, Floripas's intervention, Guy's embassy and love, and the defense of Aigremore.

contentParts.label: Book II, Part III
contentParts.summary: Sixteen chapters on Charlemagne's crossing at Mantrible, battles against Balan's forces, the recovery of relics, the conversions and marriages, and the return to France.

contentParts.label: Book III
contentParts.summary: Twenty-four chapters in two parts recount Charlemagne's Spanish and Galician campaigns, the Roncevaux betrayal and deaths, Charlemagne's final acts and death, and the source author's retrospective excuse and envoy.

contentParts.label: Book III, Part I
contentParts.summary: Fourteen chapters on Saint James's command, Charlemagne's Spanish and Galician conquests, churches, wonders, champions, and campaigns.

contentParts.label: Book III, Part II
contentParts.summary: Ten chapters on Ganelon's treason, Roncevaux, the deaths of Roland and Oliver, Ganelon's punishment, Charlemagne's laws and death, followed by the source author's excuse and envoy.

contentParts.label: Caxton's closer

locators.value: final DIV3 and TRAILER in DIV1 CharlesG:6

contentParts.summary: The unnumbered final explicit identifies William Caxton after the source author's numbered envoy and the exact translation and printing dates.

notes: The exact day is preserved in display because the schema's normalized date range is year-granular.
The exact day is preserved in display because the schema's normalized date range is year-granular.

extent.description: The intrinsic structure has a Caxton prologue, table, three books containing eighty-six numbered prose chapters, and an unnumbered closer. The XML BODY has six DIV1, eight DIV2, and eighty-seven DIV3 elements because the closer is encoded as an additional DIV3. Its ten L elements belong to Herrtage's introduction or embedded/editorial quotation and are not evidence that Caxton's prose work is mixed-form.

formStatements.description: Caxton's prologue, table, three-book narrative, source-author envoy, and closer are prose. Ten L elements in the repository XML occur in Herrtage's modern front matter or quoted/editorial material and do not make the cataloged work mixed-form.

genres.label: Chronicle romance
notes: The compilation presents legendary dynastic and sacred history through romance narrative; the label does not assert modern historical reliability.

genres.label: Crusade romance
notes: Scoped to the Holy Land expedition and the Fierabras/relic-conflict material rather than treated as the sole genre of the whole compilation.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML header's LANGUSAGE omits Middle English; direct inspection and the current incunable catalog establish it as primary.
French in Herrtage's comparisons and much of the Latin in his apparatus belong to editorial layers, not additional whole-work languages.

scope.description: Brief scriptural, liturgical, and formulaic quotations within the Middle English narrative and explicit

notes: The XML header's first title, 'Fierabras. English (Middle English) Prose.', is a legacy classification label that narrows a three-book Charlemagne compilation to one source component; it is not used as the preferred work title.

The XML's LANGUSAGE lists Latin and French but omits primary Middle English. French comparison passages and much Latin belong to Herrtage's editorial carrier; only brief Latin quotations are recorded as intrinsic quoted language.

The XML metrics—six DIV1, eight DIV2, eighty-seven DIV3, 262 PB, 376 MILESTONE, 337 NOTE1, and ten L—describe the encoding and edition. Intrinsic work extent is eighty-six numbered prose chapters plus prologue, table, and closer.

Rights and access conditions for the fifteenth-century work, physical prints and manuscripts, Herrtage editions, U-M encoding, provider images, microforms, and repository artifact remain attached to their separate lineage layers.

notes: The label describes the romance's polemical representations, warfare, and conversion episodes; it does not endorse them or identify fictional categories with modern peoples.

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy

summaries.value: Caxton's three-book prose work gathers the legendary life and campaigns of Charlemagne. Book I traces the Christianization and succession of the kings of France, Charlemagne's upbringing and rule, and his expedition to the Holy Land. Book II recounts Oliver's combat with Fierabras, Fierabras's conversion, the captivity and rescue of the Twelve Peers, Floripas's aid, the recovery of relics, and the defeat and conversion of Balan's forces. Book III follows Charlemagne's Spanish conquests, the Camino and Galician campaigns, the treason of Ganelon, the deaths of Roland and Oliver at Roncevaux, Charlemagne's final acts and death, and the source author's summary and envoy.

The work has Caxton's prologue on William Daubeny's request, a prospective table, and eighty-six numbered prose chapters arranged in three books and eight parts: Book I has 5 + 5 + 3 chapters; Book II has 16 + 17 + 16; Book III has 14 + 10. Book III's final two numbered chapters are the French source author's excuse and envoy, followed by Caxton's unnumbered explicit. The XML therefore has eighty-seven DIV3 containers: eighty-six numbered chapters plus the final closer.

The XML header's first label, 'Fierabras. English (Middle English) Prose.', describes only the best-known narrative component and is not an adequate title or boundary for this three-book compilation. Jean Bagnyon's French work is untitled in its manuscripts and is now conventionally called Histoire de Charlemagne. Caxton's exact printed French exemplar is unresolved among five candidates; Herrtage's use of the 1483 British Library print for comparison must not be converted into a direct-source claim.

notes: The spelling follows the work heading and established Herrtage/U-M title; punctuation is supplied for readability.

work.summary: Caxton's three-book prose work gathers the legendary life and campaigns of Charlemagne. Book I traces the Christianization and succession of the kings of France, Charlemagne's upbringing and rule, and his expedition to the Holy Land. Book II recounts Oliver's combat with Fierabras, Fierabras's conversion, the captivity and rescue of the Twelve Peers, Floripas's aid, the recovery of relics, and the defeat and conversion of Balan's forces. Book III follows Charlemagne's Spanish conquests, the Camino and Galician campaigns, the treason of Ganelon, the deaths of Roland and Oliver at Roncevaux, Charlemagne's final acts and death, and the source author's summary and envoy.
